title:
GERMANY AXES €15B WARSHIP PROJECT

Germany has canceled its €15.2 billion F126 frigate program and will instead buy up to eight lower-cost MEKO A-200 warships from TKMS.

The move reflects Berlin’s push to accelerate military rearmament with proven, off-the-shelf equipment.

The decision deals a blow to Rheinmetall and highlights Europe’s shift toward faster, more cost-effective defense procurement.

The move signals continued defense cooperation between Washington and Ankara despite past tensions over the F-35 program and Russian S-400 systems.

author: Nick Timiraos
title:
I think we mean different things by inertia. You're pointing at the reaction function, which might be summed up as "trend over noise, no fine-tuning." We agree; the op-ed wants more of that.

I was referring instead to the procedural kind of inertia: the multi-step ritual before a direction change. Powell in April: "we'll move to a hiking bias if we want to hike, and we'll move to a neutral bias before that." This kind of gradualism and signaling is deeply ingrained at the Fed and getting rid of it would be notable. The cartoonish example of being trapped by guidance is of course the delayed liftoff to accommodate the tapering order of operations in Q4 2021. But that's an extreme case, and there are probably other, more modest shades of it. You could argue the easing bias survived April despite several people being uncomfortable because they hadn't choreographed its exit (we could have dropped it, but it would have sent too strong a signal given how we hadn't prepared markets for it). 

What Warsh seems to want to do is remove the sense that having or not having communicated one way or the other would trap them into doing something other than what a random walk might warrant. Bernanke gave an important speech on this in 2004, outlining the benefits of "gradualism" versus a "cold turkey" approach. Guidance removal cuts the procedural inertia while raising the slower-reaction kind the op-ed wants. So it's a different axis from the inertia you're referring to in that oped. And on NFP: the "feel compelled to go" pull *is* procedural inertia, and it only exists because the old regime made priced-in paths a quasi-commitment. A Fed that never guided you toward July owes you nothing in July. "We'll be meeting in six weeks" is Warsh declining to ratify the path. The point is to break that old gravity. Whether he can sustain this if there's more volatility or in a way that doesn't start to erode your credibility (it was priced, so why didn't you do it?) is an open question.

author: Nick Timiraos
title:
Bessent tells CNBC he hopes the Fed has "an open mind" on what inflation looks like after the Iran-related energy increases have reversed

Partial transcript:

BESSENT:  I think we're going to have a high GDP economy without the traditional inflation seeping in. And as over the past few days, we've read the obituaries on Alan Greenspan. He had the foresight that in the ‘90s, productivity was about 1.5% or leading into the ‘90s, he saw that the office modernization and the internet could be a boom for non-inflationary growth. And he let the economy—I think that there was in early ’97, there was one tap-on-the-brakes rate hike. But other than that, we had the longest sustained growth period in history. And I think there's a very good chance that we could see that again.

KERNEN:  But is there at this point still an underlying inflation rate that the Fed needs to be concerned with? Would you expect it rate cuts this year or even next year, given that we're nowhere near 2%?

BESSENT:  Again, I'm not going to comment on that. But what I think is that we do need to have an open mind on the price or the inflation impact of the Iran conflict, and let's see what inflation looks like on the other side of this. And then we have an open mind that the A.I. boom could up productivity and be disinflationary, get us back down to target. What I am confident is that Kevin Warsh will do what, will take the best path to satisfy both the inflation mandate and the growth mandate. And look, he came out tough talking about the inflation.

title: Bitcoin falls under $60K, but traders anticipate 15% bounce
description: Bitcoin traders still see a BTC price relief bounce taking the market toward $70,000 despite heavy shorting and a trip toward range lows.
content:
